content.text: Master Data Management (MDM) provides a single authoritative record for each significant business entity — customer, product, location, supplier. When LLMs are grounded with data retrieved from a vector index, the quality of those responses depends critically on whether the index contains one consistent, correct record per entity or multiple conflicting versions. Without MDM, a RAG corpus for a customer service AI will contain the product description from the CRM, a slightly different description from the product catalog, and a third version from the knowledge base — and the AI will retrieve whichever version scores highest in vector similarity, producing inconsistent answers to the same question.

Applying MDM principles to grounding sources means using the MDM hub's golden records as the authoritative inputs to the RAG index, not the raw source system data. This ensures the AI retrieves a single, deduplicated, conflict-resolved record for each entity, eliminating the inconsistency problem at its root rather than trying to train the model to handle conflicting data.
Why It Matters

Inconsistent entity data in a RAG corpus creates a specific failure pattern: the AI gives different answers to the same question depending on which conflicting source document is retrieved. A user who asks 'What is the price of Product X?' on two consecutive days may receive different answers if the corpus contains the old price from one system and the new price from another. This inconsistency erodes user trust faster than any other quality issue because it makes the AI seem unreliable in a way the user can directly observe.

MDM for grounding sources also simplifies data governance: when there is exactly one golden record per entity in the AI index, data quality issues can be traced directly to the MDM hub. Data lineage is clean. Stewardship responsibility is clear. And when an entity's data changes (a product is discontinued, a policy is updated), updating the golden record in the MDM hub propagates the change to the AI system through the MDM-to-RAG sync — not through ad hoc manual updates to the index.
Benefits
Consistency: the AI retrieves exactly one version of each entity, eliminating conflicting-answer problems.
Clean lineage: AI responses can be traced to the specific golden record version that grounded them.
Simplified stewardship: one steward owns each entity in the MDM hub; changes propagate automatically.
Deduplication: entity resolution in the MDM hub removes duplicate records before they reach the AI index.
Change management: updating the golden record in MDM propagates to the AI index through the sync pipeline without manual index management.
Trade-offs
MDM prerequisite: this approach requires an operational MDM hub with golden records — a significant investment if not already in place.
Coverage gaps: MDM covers structured entity data; unstructured content (policies, FAQs, free-text articles) requires different management.
Sync latency: golden records must be synced to the RAG index with appropriate freshness; this propagation step must be monitored.
MDM scope limits: MDM complements but does not replace other content management for all document types in a RAG corpus.
MDM-RAG Integration Architecture

attrs.alt: MDM for grounding sources: source systems feed into MDM Hub (deduplication, entity resolution, survivorship), which feeds the Vector Index, which grounds the LLM. With MDM: one authoritative record per entity.

content.text: Core MDM Concepts for Grounding
Entity Resolution

Entity resolution identifies that 'Acme Corp' in the CRM, 'ACME Corporation' in the ERP, and 'Acme' in the knowledge base all refer to the same real-world entity. In the context of grounding sources, entity resolution prevents the AI index from containing three separate entries for the same organization with potentially different attributes. The entity resolution algorithm uses matching rules (exact, fuzzy, phonetic) to link records across systems and assign them a shared golden entity ID.
Survivorship Rules

When multiple source systems have data for the same entity, survivorship rules determine which source's value wins for each attribute. For example: the product name comes from the CRM (most canonical), the price comes from the ERP (updated most frequently), and the description comes from the Marketing system (most complete). Survivorship rules are critical for grounding sources because they determine what the AI actually reads — they define the AI's authoritative view of the entity.
Golden Record

The golden record is the MDM hub's output: a single, merged, authoritative record per entity that reflects the best available data from all contributing systems according to the survivorship rules. For RAG, the golden record is chunked and embedded into the vector index. The vector index points only to golden record content, never to raw source system records directly.
Mental Model: Building Code Registry

Think of MDM for grounding sources like a building code registry in a city. Multiple departments (fire, electrical, plumbing, structural) maintain their own code books with potentially different versions of the same requirement. An architect using the wrong department's book gets conflicting requirements. The building code registry is the MDM hub — it merges all departmental requirements into one authoritative code per building type. Architects (AI systems) consult only the registry, never individual department books. The registry is the single source of truth.
MDM Source Configuration

entity_type: product
description: Product catalog master data for RAG grounding

contributing_sources:
  - system: erp
    entity_id_field: product_number
    authority_tier: 1
    owns_attributes: [price, sku, inventory_status, discontinued]
    update_frequency: hourly
    
  - system: crm
    entity_id_field: product_id
    authority_tier: 2
    owns_attributes: [name, category, product_line]
    update_frequency: daily
    
  - system: marketing
    entity_id_field: mkt_product_id
    authority_tier: 3
    owns_attributes: [description, features, use_cases]
    update_frequency: weekly

entity_resolution:
  matching_fields: [sku, product_number, mkt_product_id]
  fuzzy_match_on: [name]
  fuzzy_threshold: 0.85

rag_export:
  target_index: product-knowledge-index
  sync_frequency: hourly
  chunk_strategy: attribute_grouped
  embedding_model: text-embedding-3-large
  metadata_fields: [entity_id, version, updated_at, source_ids]
Implementation
Real-World Example

A telecommunications company's GenAI customer support agent retrieved product plan information from three separate source systems: the billing system (plan prices), the network configuration system (plan features), and the website CMS (plan descriptions). All three were indexed directly into the vector store without MDM. When the company upgraded a popular plan's data allowance from 20GB to 30GB, the billing system was updated immediately, the network configuration system within 4 hours, and the website CMS 3 days later.

During those 3 days, the AI gave three different answers to 'What is the data limit on the Plus plan?' depending on which source document scored highest in retrieval. After implementing MDM-sourced golden records for product plans (billing system as authority for plan features, 1-hour MDM-to-RAG sync), the company eliminated retrieval inconsistency. Plan change propagation from billing system update to consistent AI answers dropped from 3 days to 2 hours.
Common Mistakes

Indexing raw source system data into the RAG corpus alongside MDM golden records defeats the purpose. If the vector index contains both a golden record and a raw CRM record for the same product, retrieval will sometimes return the raw record, bypassing the MDM investment. The index must contain only golden record-sourced content for entity types covered by MDM.

A second mistake is applying MDM to unstructured document types. MDM excels at structured entity master data; it is not the right tool for managing FAQ articles, policy PDFs, or support transcripts. These documents require content management, version control, and stewardship review — not entity resolution and survivorship rules.
Best Practices
Use MDM golden records as the exclusive source for entity-type data in the RAG index — never index raw source records alongside them.
Define survivorship rules explicitly per attribute, not per record — different attributes may have different authoritative sources.
Propagate golden record versions with the indexed document so that retrieved content can be traced to a specific MDM version.
Monitor MDM-to-RAG sync lag separately from other freshness metrics — sync failure is high-impact for entity data consistency.
Start MDM coverage with the highest-impact entity types (products, core policies) and expand incrementally.
Use MDM entity IDs as retrieval filter metadata to enable entity-specific queries and improve retrieval precision.
Performance and Scalability

MDM hubs are designed for structured entity queries, not bulk text generation. The MDM-to-RAG pipeline should be asynchronous: the MDM hub publishes change events when golden records are updated, and a separate indexing pipeline consumes those events, generates embeddings, and updates the vector index. This decouples MDM query performance from RAG indexing and allows each to scale independently. At large entity counts, the indexing pipeline must support incremental updates rather than full re-indexing on every change.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Informatica MDM: enterprise MDM with golden record management, entity resolution, and APIs for downstream export.
Reltio: cloud-native MDM with real-time entity resolution and REST API for golden record access.
TIBCO EBX: MDM platform with data model governance and change event publishing.
Apache Atlas: open-source metadata management with entity resolution capabilities.
Pinecone / Weaviate / Qdrant: vector databases with metadata filtering that support entity_id-based retrieval filters.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach

Complexity

Security and Reliability

MDM golden records often contain the most sensitive business data: customer PII, product pricing with competitive information, supplier contracts. The MDM-to-RAG pipeline must apply classification-based access controls: only golden record attributes cleared for AI system access should be included in the RAG export. Attributes marked as confidential, PII, or restricted must be filtered out before indexing.

The MDM sync pipeline is a critical path for AI system data quality. If the MDM hub is unavailable, the RAG index stops receiving updates — the AI continues serving from the current index but freshness degrades. Monitor MDM hub availability as a dependency of the AI system's SLA, and define the acceptable window of MDM unavailability before triggering a quality alert.
Key Takeaways
MDM golden records provide the single authoritative record per entity that eliminates conflicting AI answers to the same question.
Entity resolution and survivorship rules are the MDM mechanisms that produce golden records — define survivorship explicitly per attribute.
Index only MDM-sourced golden records for entity-type data in the RAG corpus — never alongside raw source system records.
The MDM-to-RAG sync pipeline is a critical dependency: monitor its lag and availability separately from other AI pipeline health metrics.
Apply classification-based filtering in the RAG export — confidential or PII attributes must not be indexed into the AI corpus.
MDM is the right tool for structured entity data; unstructured documents require different governance approaches.
Start with the highest-impact entity types and expand MDM coverage incrementally as the RAG corpus matures.
